message.content:
Create the file /home/wink/data/prgs/rust/histogram-no-std/examples/h2demo.rs — a demo example for the histogram-no-std crate. Modify ONLY that one new file. Do not touch src/, Cargo.toml, or any other file.

## Crate API you must use (already implemented, read src/ to confirm signatures)

- `histogram_no_std::Config` — `Config::new(grouping_power: u8, max_value_power: u8) -> Result<Config, Error>`; methods `total_buckets() -> usize`, `max_value() -> u64`, `index_for(u64) -> usize`, `value_range(usize) -> (u64, u64)`.
- `histogram_no_std::Histogram` — `Histogram::new(config, &mut [u32]) -> Result<_, Error>`, `record(&mut self, u64)`, `total() -> u64`, `quantile(f64) -> Option<u64>`, `buckets() -> impl Iterator<Item = Bucket>`.
- `histogram_no_std::Bucket` — pub fields `low: u64`, `high: u64`, `count: u64`, `cumulative: u64`.

## What the demo does

1. Config: g=7, n=30. Storage: `vec![0u32; cfg.total_buckets()]`.
2. Generate 1,000,000 deterministic synthetic latency samples (ticks) with an inline splitmix64 PRNG (no external deps; copy the shape from tests/oracle.rs), seed 42: body `base = 50 + rng % 100`; 1-in-1000 samples multiplied by `1 + rng % 10_000`; 9-in-1000 multiplied by `1 + rng % 100`; clamp to `1..=cfg.max_value()`. Record each.
3. Print an iiac-perf-style band table to stdout. Bands are defined by cumulative-fraction fences, in this order with these labels:
   z4 0.000_1, z3 0.001, z2 0.01, p10 0.10, p20 0.20, p30 0.30, p40 0.40, p50 0.50, p60 0.60, p70 0.70, p80 0.80, p90 0.90, n2 0.99, n3 0.999, n4 0.999_9, n5 0.999_99, n6 0.999_999, n7 0.999_999_9, n8 0.999_999_99
   Band k covers the recorded values whose rank r (1-based) satisfies prev_fence < r/total <= fence_k (first band starts at rank 1; the LAST band with any data extends to rank = total). Compute per band from ONE pass over `h.buckets()` using the `cumulative` field — do not call quantile() per band:
   - first = low of the first bucket whose count overlaps the band
   - last  = high of the last bucket overlapping the band
   - count = number of ranks in the band (from cumulative fences, exact integer)
   - mean  = weighted average of bucket midpoints ((low+high)/2, use f64) over the band's portion of each bucket (a bucket straddling a fence contributes only its in-band ranks)
   - skip (do not print) bands with count == 0
4. After the bands print four summary rows in the same column layout: `mean` and `stdev` over ALL samples, then `mean z4..n2` and `stdev z4..n2` over only ranks up to the 0.99 fence (i.e. excluding the >p99 tail). Compute stdev via bucket-midpoint weighted E[x^2] - mean^2 (f64, sqrt is fine — std is available in examples).
5. Column layout, right-aligned, thousands separators on integer tick values (write a small `fn commas(u64) -> String`), one decimal place on means:
   `  <label> <fraction>  <first> <last> <range> <count> <mean>` with header row `first last range count mean` — match the visual feel of this iiac-perf sample (labels left-aligned in a 4-char field, fraction printed like 0.999_999 with underscores as shown in the band list above):

```
                     first          last         range        count          mean
  z4  0.000_1         56.2          68.2          12.0          898          67.8
  ...
  n8  0.999_999_99 64,880.6      64,880.6           0.0            1      64,880.6
```
   (Numbers there are ns with decimals; ours are integer ticks — print ticks with commas, means with one decimal.)
6. Also print, before the table: a title line `h2demo — histogram-no-std band table`, then `config: g=7 n=30 buckets=<N> bytes=<N*4>`, `samples: <total>`, and p50/p99/p99.9/max on one line using `quantile()`.

## Constraints (project conventions, enforced by clippy -D warnings)

- File starts with a `//!` module doc comment (one-sentence purpose + a couple of `-` bullets). Every fn gets a `///` doc comment. Lines in doc comments wrap at ~72 cols.
- NO `.unwrap()`, `.expect()`, or `.unwrap_or*()` anywhere: `main() -> Result<(), histogram_no_std::Error>` and use `?`/`match`/`if let`. For Option in fences etc. structure code so no unwrap is needed. (The project lints unwrap_used/expect_used as warnings and CI runs clippy with -D warnings.)
- No inline `//` comments except a non-obvious WHY.
- Must pass: `cargo fmt` (run it), `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`, and `cargo run --example h2demo` must print the table without panicking.

Iterate until all three commands succeed. Your final report: state PASS/FAIL for fmt/clippy/run, and paste the first ~30 lines of the demo's stdout. Return raw data, no pleasantries.

Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. - artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact page can be granted — behavior static HTML cannot provide on its own, such as the page reading live or connected data, keeping state shared across viewers, or updating and republishing itself. Serves this user's live capability roster and the typed call definitions. Load it whenever the user asks for an artifact needing any such runtime behavior.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- claude-in-chrome: Automates your Chrome browser to interact with web pages - clicking elements, filling forms, capturing screenshots, reading console logs, and navigating sites. Opens pages in new tabs within your existing Chrome session. Requires site-level permissions before executing (configured in the extension). - When the user wants to interact with web pages, automate browser tasks, capture screenshots, read console logs, or perform any browser-based actions. Always invoke BEFORE attempting to use any mcp__claude-in-chrome__* tools.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

file.content:
# no_std-first HdrHistogram-style log-linear histogram; the
# core recording structure for ../tprobe. Design and size
# tradeoffs in ARCHITECTURE.md; version-of-record lives here
# (see notes/versioning.md).
[package]
name = "histogram-no-std"
version = "0.1.0-7"
edition = "2024"
rust-version = "1.97"
license = "MIT OR Apache-2.0"
description = "no_std no-alloc HdrHistogram-style log-linear histogram"
publish = false

[features]
default = ["std"]
std = []

# Panicking sites are opt-in: each needs a site `#[allow]` +
# `// OK: …` justification (see AGENTS.md > Code Conventions).
[lints.clippy]
unwrap_used = "warn"
expect_used = "warn"

[dependencies]

# Correctness oracles (tests only): iopsystems `histogram`
# shares the h2 scheme (exact counts parity); `hdrhistogram`
# is the reference implementation (quantile parity within
# equivalent-value tolerance).
[dev-dependencies]
hdrhistogram = { version = "7", default-features = false }
histogram = "1"

Note: clippy's `manual_unwrap_or` / `manual_unwrap_or_default` lints wanted `quantile_or_zero`'s `match` collapsed into `.unwrap_or(0)`/`.unwrap_or_default()`. Since the task forbids `unwrap_or*`, I kept the explicit `match` and added a scoped `#[allow(clippy::manual_unwrap_or, clippy::manual_unwrap_or_default)]` with an `// OK: …` comment explaining why, rather than switching to the disallowed form — flagging this per the project's alert-on-`unwrap`-adjacent-code convention even though no actual `unwrap*`/`expect`/`unwrap_or*` call exists in the file.

`n7`/`n8` bands are absent as expected (their raw fence boundaries collapse to the same rank as `n6` at this sample size, so their count is 0 and they're skipped; the "extend last band with data to total" rule correctly rolled the leftover rank into `n6`, whose `count` sums to 1,000,000 - 999,990 = 10). Cross-check: `p50`/`n2`/`n3` band `last` values (100 / 149 / 29,823) match the `quantile()`-derived p50/p99/p99.9 line exactly, confirming the single-pass band math agrees with the crate's own quantile implementation.
